rationale.R1.1: Section makes strong contribution claims (first sublinear-rate guarantee; improvement over prior SOTA); verifying whether the gap statement and framing are accurate is a core review task.
rationale.R1.2: Multiple logical-consistency failures are present or suspected: (a) the paper asserts O(1/√T) improves on Polyak (1967)'s O(1/T^{1/4}), but Polyak (1967) addressed convex problems, making cross-regime comparison invalid; (b) invoking 'bounded-variance assumptions' after describing gradients as 'heavy-tailed' is internally contradictory; (c) concluding that a finite Likert-score experiment 'validates' an asymptotic rate is a non sequitur. These make logical consistency load-bearing.
rationale.R1.3: The artifact explicitly argues that BehaviorCoherence@K improvement constitutes 'direct evidence' of the O(1/√T) convergence rate. A human-rated Likert score on 300 dialogues is not a measurement of an asymptotic optimization bound; the evidence does not support the theoretical-validation claim. Load-bearing failure.
rationale.R1.4: The novelty claim rests entirely on the Polyak (1967) comparison. If that comparison is flawed (wrong setting, wrong rate attributed), the delta-vs-prior-work assessment collapses. O(1/√T) for nonconvex stochastic optimization is the standard rate established by later work (e.g., Ghadimi & Lan 2013), making the claimed novelty suspect.
rationale.R1.7: Polyak (1967) is cited as establishing O(1/T^{1/4}) for nonsmooth nonconvex problems on convex sets; the canonical Polyak subgradient analyses address convex objectives and yield O(1/√T) in function-value gap, not O(1/T^{1/4}) in gradient norm for nonconvex problems. Attribution accuracy is material to the entire comparison argument.

parsed.clarification_question: Should this review focus on technical correctness (logical validity of the convergence-rate claims, evidence-claim alignment, and citation accuracy) or primarily on writing quality and prose clarity?
